name: Swift SIL
summary: Unusually high-level for an IR: preserves Swift-specific constructs (existentials, generic specialization markers, witness tables, ARC retain/release pairs) that LLVM IR cannot represent. SSA-form with Swift-aware operations rather than generic LLVM ones.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Swift's SIL is unusually high-level for an IR: it preserves Swift-specific constructs (existentials, generic specialization markers, witness tables, ARC retain/release pairs, devirtualization opportunities) that LLVM IR cannot represent. SIL is SSA-form but with Swift-aware operations rather than generic LLVM ones — it has `apply` (function call), `partial_apply` (closure construction), `class_method`, `witness_method`, and so on.

sections.title: Why a Swift-specific IR exists
sections.content:
Swift's optimizations (specialization, devirtualization, ARC elision, escape analysis) need representation power that LLVM lacks, and lowering to LLVM IR before running them would lose information. SIL is the optimization substrate; lowering to LLVM IR happens only after SIL-level optimizations have run.

This is a multi-level architecture without using MLIR — Swift built a bespoke high-level SSA IR before MLIR existed and continues to use it because the Swift-specific operations carry semantics that MLIR-style dialects could express but at higher engineering cost.

sections.title: ARC and generic specialization in SIL
sections.content:
ARC retain/release pairs appear as explicit SIL operations, which lets the optimizer reason about reference-counting traffic at the IR level — eliding redundant pairs, sinking releases, and identifying unique-reference paths for in-place mutation. This is the SIL-level analog of LLVM's `nounwind`/`noreturn` attribute reasoning, specialised for ARC semantics.

Generic specialization is similarly first-class at SIL level: SIL marks generic instantiations explicitly, lets the optimizer choose which ones to specialize, and records witness-table dispatch points that can later be devirtualized when the concrete type is known.
